Re: Your Request for Advice

Dear Ms. GeIb:
You are seeking advice on behalf of Councilmembers Ann
ChandIer. Shirley Dean. and Mary wainwright regarding their duties
and responsibiliaies under the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of
the Political 
Reform Act (the rrAct") 1 with respect to a pending
deci,sion before the Berkeley city council. As the authorized
representative or the councilmemLers, we provide you with advice
pursuant eo Regulation L8329.
The following advi.ce is based upon the facts provided in your
iet:er of November 8, l99l and in several telephone conversations
n" u." aLso in recej-pt of a letter, dated November 7, \g91,
irom Robert Kish, who, on beha]f of an unnamed client, 
has
expressed an i.nterest in this matter and takes issue with some of
ihe factual and legal analysis you have offered in your letter'
r.\ .:cpy cf l,!r. Kish' s letter, 
summariz ing his contentions, was
sent t; you via facsinile transmi.ssion on November 19, 1991.) The
Ccnmission does not ac+- as a finder of fact and wiII not attempt
r-o resoi're i,/hatever lactual disputes that may exist concerni-ng
ahis ::la!ter. lve reiterate the cornrnission's long-standing policy
that the advice provided in this letter is applicable only to the
extent ihe facts you have provided are correct and that all
nateri-al facts have been presented. (In re Oqlesbv (1975) 1 FPPC
cps. 71.) If the facts provided are inaccurate or otherwi'se

incomplete′  the advice may be
the adv■ce may be withdrawn.
changed or modified; if necessary,
AIso, the Comrnission does not provide advice with respect to
past conduct. (Regulation 18329.) Although you have indicated
that a first reading of the proposed ordinance has aLready
occurred, and the vote thereon included the participation of the
three councilmenbers, the ordinance has or will be anended and
returned to the council for a (new) first reading. Therefore,
this advice is prospective onIy, applicable to the forthcoming
decision, and does not constitute any guidance or counsel on
actions that have already been taken.
OUESTION
Do councilnembers Chandler, Dean, or Wainwright have a
disqualifying conflict with respect to a pending ordinance before
the city council that r{ri1l, restrict the conversion of certain
residential dwelling units to tenancies- in-common (a fractional,
undivided interest in Iand coupled t ith a right to exclusive
occupancy of a unit within the property) ?
CONCLUSION
t.Jhether one or nore of the councilmembers can participate in
the pending decision that ?iI1 restrict the conversion of certain
residenti,al dwelling units to tenanc ies- in-common depends on
whether the decision wiII foreseeably and naterially financially
affect a councilmember's property interests.
FACTS
Pending before the Berkeley city council is an ordinance
which wiII limit the sale of itenanc ies- in-comrnon, or rtTfCs. t
The ordinance defines a TIc as a fractional. undivided interest in
land coupled $rith a right to exclusive occupancy of a unit withln
the property. The right to exclusive occupancy is not included on
the face of the deed but instead is provided for in a separate
written agreement or representation. You have indicated that the
purpose of the ordinance is to restrj.ct lhe conversion of certain
residential rental dwelling units t,c TIcs for an interim period,
during $rhich the city woul-d formulate a new polj-cy regardj,ng the
depletion of housing stock.
Because the express purpose of the ordinance is to naintai.n
the status quo pending forrnulation of a new housing poli.cy,
certain exceptions are included in the proposed ordinance. Anong
the two rel,evant for purposes of this analysis are (1) the
exclusion from application of the ordinance to exj-sting TICs, and
(2) the exclusion frorn application of the ordi.nance to TICs in


File No. A-91-511
Page 3
buildings with three or fewer units.
of the three councilnembers on whose behalf this advi-ce is
being requested, only Councilmenber Ann Chandler is an owner of a
TICr' sinCe 1984 she has owned one unit of a two-unit TIc- 
Because
it is not a rental property, her property interest is not subject
to the city's rent control Laws.
Neither councilroembers Shirely Dean or Mary wainwright are
TIC olrners. Councilmember Dean is a less-than-fifty percent owner
of a two-unit duplex; because one unit is occupied by her son, who
is a 50* owner of the duplex, Councilrnenber Dean's Property
interest is not subject to the city's rent control Ia$/s. You have
indicated that should she either se1I her property interest
outright, or convert her interest into a TIc and then seLl it, the
revenue generated from either sale would be the same.
Councilmember Mary wainwright owns at least seven four-unit
rental properties. of these, some are governed by Berkeley
Housing Authority and not subject to the city's rent control laws,'
some are subject to the rent control provisions and some are
vacant.
Each of the councilmembers' financial interest in their
respective propertj.es is worth more than one thousand dollars'
You have indicated that although a first reading of the
ordinance has already occurred and that the three counci.lnembers
participated in the vote at the first reading, an amendment. to the
ordinante changing ihe duration of the proPosed moratorium has or
wilI be made, and that the ordinance sill 
be returning to the
counciL for a (new) first reading.
ANALYSI S
A public official 
is prohibited from making, participating
in, or using his or her official 
position to influence a
governmental deci.sion in which the off icial' knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A
pubJ.ic official 
has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonabi-y foreseeable that the decision wiII have a material
financial effect, dj. stinguishable fron its effect on the public
generaliy, on - among other things - any real property in v,hich
ine of f i.iial has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
(S1,000) or nore. (Section 371O3(b) .)
As elected members of the BerkeJ.ey City council, the three
councilmembers are public officials 
(section 82o48) and nust
disqualify thernsel.res from any governmental decj.sions which wilI
have a reasonably foreseeable material financiaL effect,
d istinguishabl.e from the public general.Iy, on their real property
interests. 
(Section 87103 (b) . )


File No. A-9■-5■■
Page 4
ForeseeablE uaterial, PiuaDcial Effoct
Foreseeab i I itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial Likelihood that it wiII occur. whil-e certainty
is not required, an effect that is nerely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Dolrnev community
Development com. (f987) L96 cal.App.3d 983; fn re lhorner (1975)
l FPPC ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrow
(L977\ 7o ca1.App.3d 817 , 823.)
To the extent that the proposed ordinance will have an inpact
on housing stock in the jurisdiction, 
it appears that aII three
counc i lrnernbers have ownershj.p interests in property that, in one
form or another, wilI be foreseeably affected by the Proposed
ordinance. The foreseeability factors particular to each
councilmember are additionally addressed bel.ow. whether the
effect is material, and therefore disqualifyinq, requires separate
analysis.
Materia I itv
Councilmember chandler has an ownership interest in a TIC
that, because of an express exception provided for in the proposed
ordinance, is exempted from the ordj.nance.
councilmember Dean has no ownership interest in a TIc. 
she
is, however, a part (Iess than 5o*) owner of a duplex that,
because of an express exception provided for in the proposed
ordinance, is exenpted from the ordinance.
Councilnember wainwright has no ownership interest in a TIc
but owns at least seven rental properties, none of which appear to
be exempted from the ordinance.
None of the three councilmembers' econonic i-nterests is
directly involved in the pending decision on the proposed
ordinance. None of the councilmernbers or their respective
economic interests has initj.ated the proceeding in which the
decision will be made, or is a named party in the proceeding, or
is seeking a Iicense, pernit, or other entitlenent. 
(Regulation
L8'702.1 (b) .) 
!.rhen an of f ici.al's property interest is indirectly
involved in a decision, materiality is to be eval-uated under
Regulation la7 02 .3 .
Because the proposed ordinance will apply a city-wide
moratorium on Tfc conversions, there is no particular property or
properties which can be considered the subject of the decisj.on,


File No. A-91-511
Page 5
and the 'rdistance teststr of Regulation 18702.3(a) and (b) are not
applicabJ.e. fnstead, materiality rril.l be determined based upon
the foreseeable effect in terns of dollars on the counci lrnembers'
properties, pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(c). (EIan Advice
Letter, No. I-89-457.) 
A councilDember will thus have a
disqualifying conflict if as a result of the decision on the
proposed ordinance, her property interest wil.l be affected by
Li-ttrer ten thousand dollars (S10,ooo) or nore in fair market value
(Regutation 1-8702.3 (a) (3) (a)) or one thousand dollars ($1,000) in

whether the effect of the decision is positive or negative is of
no consequence under the Act.

149. )
counci lnenber chandler
councilmember chandler's ownership interest in her duplex TIC
is currently valued at S14o,ooo. Because she has maintained and
Lived in this interest since 1984, it is not affected by the
proposed moratorium.2 Under Regulation L8702.3(c), her
participation in the proposed ordinance decision would be
precluala under the Act's con f I ict-of- interest provisions - if the
ordinance would affect her interest by S1o,ooo, or approximately
7.lZ of the va.Lue of her ownership interest.
In the appraisal you have provided, the appraiser has
declined to opine as to the precise dollar value of irnpact on

existing TIcs, the appraisal obtained apparently concludes that
"little 
noticeablerr financial impact wiIl result from a single

Counc i Inernber Dean

Because the building has three or fevrer units it is not

You have previously indicated that the market value of

appears to be bas6d on the premise that as a part owner-occupied

The appraj.sal you have provided does not resolve the

inswer,' was lacking. Instead, the apprai.sal suggests that because
the proposed noratoriun is intended for a limited duration. rrthere
wilI- prtbably be Iittle 
noticeable impact, especially with the
current receision expected to continue 'erell' into L992''l

on TIc conversions wil-l have the Likely result of enhancing the
(property) value of existing properties strlt eligible for TIc
ctnvlrsj.on under the exceptj.on for three-or-fewer unit buiidings,
such as council-menber Dean's, whether such an enhancernent will-
rise to the level of the financial thresholds provided for in
Regulation L8702.3 is a factual deternlnation.

sinilar to the discussion infra, although a complete and

Councilmernber wainwriqht

lppraiser's conclusion that "there wilI probably be Iittle

residential dwelling units to tenancies- in-common (a fractional,

occupancy of a unit within the property) ?

residenti,al dwelling units to tenanc ies- in-common depends on
whether the decision wiII foreseeably and naterially financially
